original_abstract: This study sought to identify the core knowledge and competencies essential for executive leadership in higher education from the perspective of sitting university presidents. Through in-depth interviews, thirteen presidents—all holding doctoral degrees in higher education administration—reflected on the abilities required to navigate the complexities of the 21st-century presidency. The participants categorized essential knowledge into three primary domains: foundational (history and finance), cultural (context and public image), and complex cognitive thinking. In terms of practical competencies, the respondents emphasized the importance of personal attributes, management (including fundraising, enrollment, and strategic planning), and multifaceted communication skills. The findings reveal that modern presidents view their roles as a hybrid of visionary leadership and pragmatic management, suggesting that specialized academic preparation and professional development are vital for success in these high-visibility positions.
humanized_abstract: What Knowledge and Skills do Executive Leaders in Higher Education Need to Succeed? Presidents’ Perspectives As the landscape of higher education continued to shift in the 21st century, there was a growing concern about what skills and knowledge might be needed for a new generation of executive leaders. The study reported in this report attempted to answer this question by asking sitting university presidents to reflect on their experience as leaders and to share their insights about their skills and knowledge. Thirteen sitting university presidents with doctoral degrees in higher education administration were interviewed for the study and reflected on the skills and knowledge they believed they needed to do their 21st-century-presidency work effectively. They identified knowledge necessary to practice as an executive leader in the form of three pillars: the foundational (historical and financial), the cultural (contextual and to do with public image), and complex cognitive thinking. They identified skills in the form of personal skills, management skills (which included fund raising, recruitment, and planning and policy), and communication skills. This study of the world’s largest group of executive leaders in higher education revealed that these presidents are seen as not only being dynamic leaders, but also effective managers. Thus, they believe that specialized higher education for administrative leadership is vital to ensuring that they are prepared for their critical leadership roles in public eyes.
